name: enact-hello-brainfuck
description: A greeting tool written entirely in Brainfuck - the esoteric programming language
capabilities: Executes Brainfuck code to output greeting messages
Returns structured JSON output with the greeting message
Demonstrates Brainfuck esolang syntax and programming concepts
Provides educational examples of Brainfuck program structure
author: EnactProtocol
securitySummary: Static scanner flagged 17 external_command patterns and 4 weak_crypto patterns. Manual review confirms all are false positives. The external_command detections are Brainfuck language syntax (>, <, +, -, ., ,, [, ]) documented in SKILL.md, not shell commands. The weak_crypto detections are text containing 'Brainfuck' misidentified by the scanner. This skill contains only documentation and a Brainfuck source file - no executable code or security-sensitive operations.
valueStatement: Brainfuck is an esoteric programming language with only 8 commands. This skill provides a complete Hello World implementation demonstrating minimalist code that outputs greetings using pure ASCII manipulation.
limitations: Only outputs predefined greeting messages from static Brainfuck code
Does not accept user input for dynamic message generation
Limited to Brainfuck interpreter functionality within timeout constraints
No file system or network access capabilities

useCases.title: Learn Brainfuck Syntax
useCases.description: New programmers explore the Brainfuck esolang by examining how a complete program uses only 8 commands to produce output.

useCases.title: Demonstrate Esoteric Languages
useCases.description: Educators show Brainfuck as an example of a Turing-complete language with minimal instruction set for teaching computation concepts.

useCases.title: Tool Registry Showcase
useCases.description: Developers add a playful example to their tool registry demonstrating that even impractical languages have practical applications.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Greeting
promptTemplates.prompt: Run enact/hello-brainfuck to get a greeting
promptTemplates.scenario: Simple request to execute the Brainfuck hello world program

promptTemplates.title: Brainfuck Example
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how enact/hello-brainfuck works and what it outputs
promptTemplates.scenario: Learning about the skill's functionality and output format

promptTemplates.title: Esolang Demonstration
promptTemplates.prompt: Run the Brainfuck skill and explain how the program produces its output using only the 8 Brainfuck commands
promptTemplates.scenario: Understanding Brainfuck programming concepts through execution

promptTemplates.title: Compare Outputs
promptTemplates.prompt: Run enact/hello-brainfuck and explain the JSON response structure it returns
promptTemplates.scenario: Examining the skill's output format for integration purposes

bestPractices: Use this skill for educational purposes to understand minimalist programming languages
Review the source hello.bf file to see how complex output is produced with simple commands
Combine with documentation reading skills to understand Brainfuck esolang principles
antiPatterns: Do not expect this skill to accept dynamic input or generate custom messages
Avoid assuming this skill has network or file system access
Do not use this skill for production code - it is an educational demonstration

faq.question: What is Brainfuck?
faq.answer: Brainfuck is an esoteric programming language created in 1993 by Urban Müller. It uses only 8 commands and is designed to be minimal while remaining Turing-complete.

faq.question: What does this skill actually do?
faq.answer: It executes a pre-written Brainfuck program that outputs 'Hello from Brainfuck!' and returns the message in JSON format.

faq.question: Can I modify the greeting message?
faq.answer: No. This skill runs a fixed Brainfuck program. To change the output, you would need to modify the underlying hello.bf file.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill runs Brainfuck code with a 30-second timeout and has no network or file system access beyond its defined operation.

faq.question: What are the 8 Brainfuck commands?
faq.answer: The commands are: > (move right), < (move left), + (increment), - (decrement), . (output), , (input), [ (jump forward if zero), ] (jump back if non-zero).

faq.question: Why does this skill exist?
faq.answer: Every tool registry deserves at least one Brainfuck program. It demonstrates that even impractical-looking languages have practical implementations and can teach fundamental programming concepts.
